content:
# Acts Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 227-228 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_003_page_001.jpg, ACT_003_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now Peter and John were going up to the temple at the hour of prayer, the ninth hour. **2** And a certain man, lame from his mother's womb, was being carried, whom they placed daily at the gate of the temple called Beautiful, to ask alms from those entering the temple. **3** He, seeing Peter and John about to enter the temple, was asking to receive alms. **4** And Peter, gazing at him with John, said, "Look at us." **5** And he fixed his attention on them, expecting to receive something from them. **6** But Peter said, "Silver and gold I do not possess, but what I have, this I give to you: in the name of Jesus Christ the Nazarene, rise up and walk." **7** And seizing him by the right hand, he raised him up; and immediately his feet and ankles were strengthened. **8** And leaping up, he stood and began to walk, and he entered the temple with them, walking and leaping and praising God. **9** And all the people saw him walking and praising God. **10** And they recognized him, that this was the one who sat for alms at the Beautiful Gate of the temple, and they were filled with wonder and astonishment at what had happened to him.

**11** And while he was holding fast to Peter and John, all the people ran together to them at the portico called Solomon's, greatly astonished. **12** And when Peter saw this, he responded to the people, "Men of Israel, why do you marvel at this, or why do you gaze at us, as though by our own power or godliness we had made him walk? **13** The God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob, the God of our fathers, glorified his servant Jesus, whom you delivered up and denied in the presence of Pilate, when he had decided to release him. **14** But you denied the Holy and Righteous One, and asked for a murderer to be granted to you, **15** and you killed the Author of life, whom God raised from the dead, of which we are witnesses. **16** And upon the basis of faith in his name, his name has strengthened this man whom you see and know; and the faith that is through him has given him this perfect health in the presence of you all.

**17** "And now, brothers, I know that you acted in ignorance, just as your rulers also did. **18** But what God foretold through the mouth of all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer, he thus fulfilled. **19** Repent therefore and turn back, so that your sins may be blotted out, **20** that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord, and that he may send the Christ appointed for you — Jesus, **21** whom heaven must receive until the times of restoration of all things, of which God spoke through the mouth of his holy prophets from of old. **22** Moses indeed said, 'The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet from your brothers, like me. You shall listen to him in all things, whatever he speaks to you. **23** And it shall be that every soul that does not listen to that prophet shall be utterly destroyed from the people.' **24** And all the prophets, from Samuel and those who followed after, as many as spoke, also proclaimed these days. **25** You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant which God made with your fathers, saying to Abraham, 'And in your seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed.' **26** To you first, God, having raised up his servant, sent him to bless you, in turning each one of you from your wickedness."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.2 — "Beautiful" (Ωραιαν):** The gate called Ωραια ("Beautiful/Lovely") has been variously identified with the Nicanor Gate or the Shushan Gate. The adjective derives from ωρα ("season, prime"), conveying beauty in the sense of being "in full bloom." Rendered simply "Beautiful" as a proper name following established convention.

2. **v.6 — "rise up and walk" (εγειρε και περιπατει):** The present imperatives convey both an immediate command and a continuing state. Sinaiticus reads εγειρε (intransitive "rise up") rather than the variant εγειραι. The command echoes the healing formula found in the Synoptic Gospels (cf. Mark 2:9).

3. **v.7 — "feet and ankles" (βασεις και σφυδρα):** Luke employs precise medical terminology here. The word βασεις refers to the bases or soles of the feet, and σφυδρα (a hapax in the NT) denotes the ankle bones. Luke's medical vocabulary is consistent with the tradition identifying him as a physician (Col 4:14).

4. **v.13 — "servant" (παιδα):** The word παις can mean "child" or "servant." Here it echoes the Servant Songs of Isaiah (especially Isaiah 52:13 LXX, where the same word is used of the Lord's servant). Rendered "servant" consistently in vv. 13 and 26 to preserve the Isaianic allusion. This is distinct from δουλος ("slave/bondservant").

5. **v.14 — "the Holy and Righteous One" (τον αγιον και δικαιον):** Two substantival adjectives functioning as christological titles. "The Holy One" (ο αγιος) echoes Psalm 16:10 (LXX 15:10), cited by Peter in Acts 2:27. "The Righteous One" (ο δικαιος) anticipates Stephen's usage in Acts 7:52. Both are rendered with capital letters as recognized titles.

6. **v.15 — "Author of life" (αρχηγον της ζωης):** The term αρχηγος carries the sense of "originator," "pioneer," or "prince/leader." It recurs in Acts 5:31 ("Prince and Savior") and Hebrews 2:10; 12:2. Rendered "Author" to convey the sense of originator and source, while "Prince" or "Pioneer" are defensible alternatives.

7. **v.16 — "upon the basis of faith in his name" (επι τη πιστει του ονοματος αυτου):** The genitive του ονοματος may be objective ("faith directed toward his name") or subjective. The prepositional phrase επι τη πιστει indicates the ground or basis of the healing. The verse is syntactically complex, with "his name" as both instrument and object of faith.

8. **v.19 — "turn back" (επιστρεψατε):** The aorist imperative επιστρεψατε ("turn back/return") is paired with μετανοησατε ("repent"). Together these two verbs capture the full scope of biblical repentance: a change of mind (μετανοια) issuing in a concrete turning (επιστροφη) toward God.

9. **v.20 — "times of refreshing" (καιροι αναψυξεως):** The word αναψυξις (a NT hapax) means literally "a cooling, a breathing space, refreshment." It denotes the eschatological relief and restoration that flows from the presence of the Lord. Rendered "refreshing" to preserve the distinctiveness of this rare term.

10. **v.21 — "times of restoration of all things" (χρονων αποκαταστασεως παντων):** The word αποκαταστασις ("restoration, re-establishment") appears only here in the NT. It carries the sense of the prophesied renewal of all things. Note that Luke uses χρονων ("times," from χρονος, denoting duration) rather than καιροι ("appointed seasons") from v. 20 — a deliberate distinction.

11. **v.22 — Moses' prophecy (Deut 18:15-16, 18-19):** Peter conflates Deuteronomy 18:15 with 18:19, linking the promise of a prophet "like Moses" to the warning of judgment upon those who refuse to hear. Sinaiticus reads the text in essential agreement with the LXX of Deuteronomy. The phrase "from your brothers" (εκ των αδελφων υμων) specifies the prophet's Israelite origin.

12. **v.23 — "utterly destroyed" (εξολεθρευθησεται):** This strong compound verb (εξ + ολεθρευω) intensifies the concept of destruction. The future passive indicates divine judgment. The phrase "from the people" (εκ του λαου) echoes the covenant-exclusion formula of Leviticus 23:29 (LXX), where the same verb appears.

13. **v.25 — "families" (πατριαι):** Sinaiticus reads πατριαι ("families, clans") rather than the variant εθνη ("nations") found in some manuscripts, following the LXX of Genesis 12:3 / 22:18. Rendered "families" to reflect the Sinaiticus reading faithfully.

14. **v.26 — "having raised up" (αναστησας):** This verb αναστημι can mean either "raised up" in the sense of "brought onto the scene" (i.e., sending his servant into ministry) or "raised from the dead" (resurrection). The context here favors the former sense — God raised up (appointed/sent) his servant to Israel first — though a deliberate double meaning echoing the resurrection cannot be excluded.

— LOGOS
